meta.title: flect, flex

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 2 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: flect, flex
root.mentor_note: flect and flex both mean bend, and English keeps flex as a living, everyday word on its own.

attempt.direction_prompt: Rigid and unable to adjust, or adaptable and easily bent?

context_sentences: The new contract offered flexible hours, letting staff choose their own start time.
Bamboo bends in a storm precisely because it stays flexible where a stiffer tree would simply snap.
A single inflection in her voice told him the answer was no before she said a word.
The grammar chapter covered noun inflection: how a word bends to show number or case.
Every visitor to the shrine was expected to genuflect briefly at the entrance.
He genuflected out of habit, though he had not set foot in a church for years.

construct_options.text: To bend one knee to the ground as a gesture of respect
To speak with a sudden change in tone of voice
To refuse to bend or show respect at all

context_sentences: The goalkeeper managed to deflect the shot just wide of the post at the last instant.
Every question about the delay was neatly deflected back onto the previous management team.

construct_options.text: To cause something to bend or turn aside from its path
To bend one knee as a gesture of respect
To adapt readily and easily to a change
